data.title: S&P 500 Rises to Record High
data.description:
Heavyweight tech stocks carried US equity indices on an otherwise negative session on Tuesday on mostly a positive earnings prospective in the sector.

The S&P 500 rose 0.5% to set a fresh record and the Nasdaq 100 jumped 1%.

Microsoft and Apple gained over 2% ahead of their earnings this week, while Micron and Broadcom advanced between 6% and 4% to extend the positive momentum for chip producers.

In turn, General Motors jumped more than 5% after raising its 2026 guidance, while UPS added 2% following an earnings and revenue beat.

In contrast, the Dow Jones fell about 500 points, dragged lower by a 20% plunge in UnitedHealth shares after the company warned of its first annual revenue decline in three decades.

The healthcare sector was already under pressure, with CVS Health sliding 9.7% and Humana dropping 18.8% after the US proposed holding payments to private Medicare plans flat next year.

Boeing also weighed on the index, falling 1.2% after its results failed to impress investors.

data.title: American, United Turf War Could Be Felt Outside Chicago — Market Talk
data.description: The battle between American Airlines and United Airlines for Chicago could have impacts felt beyond O'Hare, Melius Research analyst Conor Cunningham writes in a note. American, which reported earnings Tuesday, says it hopes to get back to pre-pandemic levels of 500 to 550 flights daily out of ORD; United said the same morning that it's targeting 750 flights a day from Chicago this summer, and has vowed not to cede any more gates to American. "The biggest risk we see to American in 2026 is a full-on fare war in Chicago that gradually impacts other highly competitive markets," Cunningham writes. "If history is any guide, competitive skirmishes are rarely contained." Other airlines could reduce supply in Chicago as American and United become dominant players, he adds. (elias.schisgall@wsj.com)

data.title: Starbucks Files CEO Pay Drop as Turnaround Takes Shape
data.description:
Starbucks Corp. has disclosed that Chief Executive Officer Brian Niccol received total compensation of $31 million in fiscal 2025, a notable step down from the prior year when his package was inflated by large stock incentives tied to his move from Chipotle Mexican Grill Inc. A regulatory filing released Monday shows that Niccol's latest compensation included a $5 million bonus and nearly $20 million in stock awards, some of which remain linked to company performance. By contrast, his fiscal 2024 pay totaled $96 million, driven primarily by a stock award exceeding $90 million that accompanied his recruitment.

Niccol stepped into the role in September 2024 with a mandate to reset the growth trajectory of a global coffee chain operating tens of thousands of stores across 80 countries. His turnaround strategy, branded Back to Starbucks, has centered on restoring the company's core identity by improving service levels, trimming menu complexity, and reshaping stores with softer lighting and more inviting seating. Early signs of progress have begun to emerge, though the effort has possibly not yet been sufficient to fully shift investor sentiment.

That hesitation is reflected in the share price, with Starbucks stock down 7.7% in 2025, marking a fourth consecutive annual decline and limiting Niccol's ability to earn performance-based compensation. Operationally, the most recent quarter delivered comparable sales growth for the first time in a year and a half, supported by strength in international markets. Attention now turns to first-quarter earnings scheduled for Wednesday morning, followed by an investor presentation on Thursday, where the company is expected to outline financial targets for the first time since Niccol assumed the top job.

data.title: Germany pushes for 'two-speed' Europe with new bloc of six leading economies
data.description:
Key points:
  • Germany proposes 'two-speed EU' to enhance decision-making
  • Klingbeil invites France, Poland, Spain, Italy, Netherlands
  • Plan includes capital markets union, defence, raw materials

By Christian Kraemer and Maria Martinez

Germany will push for a "two-speed" European Union to break decision-making inertia in the 27-member bloc and galvanise its economies, calling for a core group of member states to move ahead on key policies to make Europe stronger and more independent.

"Now is the time for a Europe of two speeds," German Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil said at an event hosted by Welt on Tuesday in Berlin.

Finance ministers from Germany and France want to strengthen competitiveness within the EU by introducing a new format of the bloc's six leading economies, a letter from the German minister seen by Reuters on Tuesday showed.

In the letter, Klingbeil invited partners in France, Poland, Spain, Italy and the Netherlands to a video conference on Wednesday to set an ambitious and concrete agenda to strengthen the sovereignty, resilience and competitiveness of Europe.

EU economies are trying to reduce their dependence on imported critical raw materials from countries including China and to tackle fears that trade tariffs and the fragmentation of global markets could undermine growth and investment.

"To survive in an increasingly unpredictable geopolitical situation, Europe must become stronger and more resilient," Klingbeil wrote in the letter to his counterparts dated Monday, adding that continuing as before could not be an option.

The invitation said the meeting on Wednesday is meant as a "kick-off," and a follow-up meeting in person on the margins of the next Eurogroup should follow.

CONCRETE AGENDA TO STRENGTHEN EUROPE

The letter seen by Reuters includes a four-point plan on how to push forward the capital markets union, strengthen the euro, better coordinate investment in defence and secure raw materials.

Klingbeil said the coalition of countries needs to move faster on the Savings and Investment Union to create better financing conditions for European businesses and in particular Europe’s start-ups and scale-ups.

The second point focuses on the international role of the euro as a safe haven based on predictability and the rule of law, Klingbeil said, calling for cuts to red tape and enhancing sovereignty in the payment sphere.

Regarding defence spending, Klingbeil called for better cooperation among member states and for firmly embedding defence as a priority in the next EU multiannual budget, "turning defence into an engine for growth."

To secure strategic rare earth supplies, the German minister said efforts to strengthen supply chain resilience for critical minerals must be intensified, with greater strategic engagement with international partners.

data.title: French government survives first no-confidence vote on expenditure part of 2026 budget
data.description:
The French government survived a first vote of no-confidence in parliament on Tuesday over its decision to ram through the expenditure part of the 2026 budget without giving the National Assembly the final say.

The motion, filed by the hard left France Unbowed party (LFI), was backed by 267 members of parliament. 289 votes were needed for the motion to pass.

The government last week survived two no-confidence votes over the income part of the 2026 budget.

data.title: Amazon to Shut Down All Amazon Go and Amazon Fresh Stores — WSJ
data.description:
By Joseph De Avila

Amazon is closing all of its Amazon Go and Amazon Fresh physical stores in a shift to focus on its online same-day delivery service.

The e-commerce giant said Tuesday it would convert some Amazon Go and Fresh locations into Whole Foods Market stores. Amazon's same-day delivery service for groceries is currently available in more than 5,000 U.S. cities and towns. The company said it plans to expand the service to more communities in 2026 but didn't specify where.

Amazon said it planned to open over 100 new Whole Foods Market stores over the next few years.

Updates to follow as news develops.

Write to Joseph De Avila at joseph.deavila@wsj.com

data.title: QNB Corp. Reports Earnings for Fourth Quarter 2025
data.description:
QNB Corp., the parent company of QNB Bank, has released its financial results for the fourth quarter of 2025. The company reported a net income of $3.98 million, or $1.06 per diluted share, marking a significant increase from the $3.05 million, or $0.83 per diluted share, reported in the same period of 2024. For the full year ending December 31, 2025, QNB Corp. reported a net income of $14.09 million, or $3.78 per diluted share, compared to $11.45 million, or $3.12 per diluted share, in 2024.

Financial Highlights

QNB Corp. demonstrated strong financial performance in the fourth quarter of 2025, with notable improvements in several key metrics:

  • Net interest income for the quarter was $14.04 million, up from $10.98 million in the same period of 2024.
  • Net interest margin increased to 2.95% from 2.38% in the fourth quarter of 2024.
  • Total assets as of December 31, 2025, were $1.91 billion, compared to $1.87 billion at the end of 2024.
  • Loans receivable grew by 3.8% to $1.26 billion.
  • Total deposits increased by 0.9% to $1.64 billion.

Business and Operational Highlights

The bank's operating performance improved significantly, driven by an increase in net interest income and non-interest income. The interest margin improvement led to a $3.11 million increase in net interest income, while non-interest income rose by $227,000. However, these gains were partially offset by a $1.05 million increase in non-interest expenses.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

On September 23, 2025, QNB Corp. announced a definitive agreement to acquire The Victory Bancorp, Inc. in an all-stock transaction. This merger is expected to create a bank holding company with nearly $2.4 billion in assets. The transaction is anticipated to close in the second quarter of 2026, pending regulatory and shareholder approvals.

Management's Perspective

Dave Freeman, President and CEO of QNB Corp., commented, "We are proud to report another quarter of improved operating performance, highlighted by record net interest income, strong margin expansion, and continued loan growth. These results demonstrate the resilience of our customers and the dedication of our team. The upcoming merger with Victory Bancorp represents a pivotal step forward, enabling us to deepen our community impact and create greater opportunities for our shareholders and customers alike."

Future Outlook

The company expects the merger with Victory Bancorp to be completed in the second quarter of 2026, subject to regulatory and shareholder approvals. This strategic move is anticipated to enhance the company's market position and financial performance in the coming years.

Original SEC Filing: QNB CORP. [ QNBC ] - 8-K - Jan. 27, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Community Bancorp. Reports Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2025 Financial Results
data.description:
Community Bancorp., the parent company of Community National Bank, has released its financial results for the fourth quarter and full year ending December 31, 2025. The company reported a significant increase in earnings, driven by strong loan growth and higher yields.

Financial Highlights

For the fourth quarter of 2025, Community Bancorp. reported consolidated earnings of $4.6 million or $0.83 per share, marking a 13.10% increase from the $4.1 million or $0.73 per share reported for the same period in 2024. Full year earnings for 2025 were $17 million or $3.01 per share, compared to $12.8 million or $2.28 per share for the prior year, reflecting a 32.93% increase.

Key financial metrics include a return on average assets of 1.41% for the year and 1.49% for the fourth quarter, and a net interest margin of 3.68% for the year and 3.81% for the fourth quarter. The efficiency ratio improved slightly to 57.0% for the year and 56.5% for the quarter.

Business and Operational Highlights

Total assets for the company increased by $38.6 million to $1.29 billion, a 3.09% year-over-year growth. The gross loan portfolio grew by $37 million, or 4.02%, and deposit balances increased by $69 million, or 6.89%, compared to the same period in 2024. The company's securities portfolio decreased by 9.50% to $144 million, as cash flow from maturing securities was used to fund loan growth.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

Equity capital increased to $113.7 million, with a book value per share of $20.36 as of December 31, 2025. The company completed the optional redemption of all fifteen outstanding shares of its Series A Fixed-to-Floating Rate Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock in the fourth quarter of 2025.

The company declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.25 per share payable on February 1, 2026, to shareholders of record as of January 15, 2026.

Management's Perspective

President and CEO Christopher Caldwell commented on the results, highlighting the company's strong performance and disciplined approach to managing the loan and deposit sides of the balance sheet. He emphasized the company's focus on credit quality, efficient use of capital, and maximizing shareholder return. Caldwell also expressed gratitude to the dedicated team and loyal stakeholders for their support and contributions to the company's growth and progress.

Future Outlook

The company remains optimistic about its future, with a focus on maintaining strong financial performance and customer service. As Community National Bank celebrates its 175th anniversary, the leadership team is confident in their ability to navigate the economic environment and continue delivering solid financial results.

Original SEC Filing: COMMUNITY BANCORP /VT [ CMTV ] - 8-K - Jan. 27, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: US judiciary may not be able to fully maintain operations past Feb. 4 in government shutdown
data.description:
By Nate Raymond

The federal courts may not be able to fully maintain paid operations beyond February 4 if Congress fails to prevent a partial government shutdown by passing funding legislation for the judicial branch and federal agencies, including the U.S. Department of Homeland Security, by midnight on Friday, a top judge has warned.

Judge Robert Conrad, the director of the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts, shared that assessment in an internal memo on Monday afternoon as Senate Democrats vowed to oppose a funding bill for DHS after immigration officers on Saturday fatally shot a U.S. citizen, Alex Pretti, in Minnesota.

The legislation funding the department, which oversees U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, is part of a six-bill spending package covering various areas of the federal government, including defense, health, transportation, education, and housing for the fiscal year ending September 30.

Those bills had already cleared the Republican-led U.S. House of Representatives and include a measure that would provide the judiciary $9.2 billion, up 6.4% from a year earlier. The Homeland Security bill also provides an extra $30 million for Supreme Court security.

The funding package had appeared to be on track for passage in the Senate, which had already approved bills to fund other parts of the government, until Saturday, when immigration officers shot and killed Pretti.

His death prompted Democrats in the Republican-led Senate to vow not to support further DHS funding without reforms to safeguard Americans, raising the chances of parts of the government shuttering just months after the previous record shutdown ended in November.

"The combined bill no longer appears to have sufficient votes to pass the Senate, and a lapse of some duration is a distinct possibility while a strategy to address DHS funding is developed," Conrad wrote in Monday's memo to judges nationwide.

If approved funding lapses, the judiciary would not immediately shut down and employees should report to work as normal on February 2, Conrad wrote. But he said it can only rely on fees and other balances to sustain paid operations through February 4.

That would be a very short period compared to the most recent shutdown, when the judiciary was able to sustain paid operations for over two weeks after the lapse in appropriations began on October 1 before curtailing operations and furloughing employees.

Read more:

Shutdown looms as ICE shootings spawn partisan fight over DHS funding in US Congress

US court security, public defenders to receive funding boost in spending bill

US courts set to run out of money, begin furloughs as shutdown lingers
